attributes.stem:
A previously well 30-year-old woman presents with a three-day history of intermittent fever, chills and generalised fatigue. She returned 12 days ago from a three week trip to Zimbabwe and wonders if she might have picked up something when travelling. She had all necessary vaccinations prior to her travel.

On examination she appears tired but is fully conscious and oriented. She has a temperature of 38.2°C, blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, pulse 88/min and respiratory rate 18/min. Biochemistry shows mild anaemia and thrombocytopaenia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The given scenario is most consistent with the features of malaria. Malaria is a mosquito-borne infectious disease caused by any of the five plasmodium parasites (vivax, ovale, malariae, falciparum, knowlesi). It commonly presents with symptoms of intermittent fever (usually with a pattern of fever and chills), fatigue, headache, body aches, arthralgia, myalgia and sometimes gastrointestinal symptoms. The typical incubation period is 7-30 days, though can differ with the subtype of malaria. Malaria can cause anaemia and thrombocytopaenia, which are consistent with the patient's laboratory findings. Considering the travel history to Africa and the symptoms of intermittent fever and fatigue, malaria is a likely diagnosis.

Enteric fever, including both typhoid and paratyphoid fever, is caused by Salmonella typhi and Salmonella paratyphi bacteria, respectively. It is typically acquired by consuming contaminated food or water. Symptoms include sustained high fever, headache, abdominal pain, initial constipation followed by diarrhoea, and general malaise. Given the travel history, enteric fever is an important consideration in the differential diagnosis. However, the absence of gastrointestinal symptoms in this patient make enteric fever a less likely diagnosis.

Dengue is a viral infection transmitted by the bite of infected mosquitoes. It commonly presents with a high fever, severe headache (typically behind the eyes), joint and muscle pain, rash, and fatigue. Thrombocytopaenia is a characteristic laboratory finding in dengue fever. Dengue fever can be diagnosed in patients with 2 or more clinical findings in addition to a febrile illness. This patient does not have additional findings consistent with a diagnosis, so dengue is less likely to be the cause. Additionally, the incubation period of dengue is usually 5-7 days.

Yellow fever is a viral disease caused by the flavivirus, and is transmitted by the bite of infected mosquitoes. It is primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions of South America and Africa, though not as far south as Zimbabwe. The incubation period is typically 3-6 days. Symptoms usually include headache, muscle and joint aches, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, and jaundice. Severe cases can lead to organ failure, bleeding, and shock.

Tuberculosis (TB) is an infectious disease caused by mycobacterium tuberculosis. It initially affects the respiratory system, though can become disseminated to other organs in patients that are immunocompromised. Tuberculosis is an air-borne infection and is most commonly spread to close or prolonged contacts, specifically in crowded households. It presents with a persistent cough, haemoptysis, fatigue, weight loss, fever, night sweats. Given the absence of any pulmonary symptoms, tuberculosis is a less likely cause of this patient's current problems.
learningPoints: The possibility of malaria should always be suspected in unwell individuals with a history of recent or remote travel to an endemic area.
Malaria can present with intermittent fever, chills, and fatigue, and should be considered in the differential diagnosis of febrile illnesses in returning travelers.
references: Buck E, Finnigan NA. Malaria. [Updated 2023 Jan 22]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK551711/

Tangpukdee N, Duangdee C, Wilairatana P, Krudsood S. Malaria diagnosis: a brief review. Korean J Parasitol. 2009 Jun;47(2):93-102. doi: 10.3347/kjp.2009.47.2.93. Epub 2009 May 26. PMID: 19488414; PMCID: PMC2688806.
Shahbodaghi SD, Rathjen NA. Malaria: Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment. Am Fam Physician. 2022 Sep;106(3):270-278. PMID: 36126008.
